author: Sofia Martínez
title: How Staffing Agencies Win 40% More Placements With Paid Search
description: Stop competing on price alone. We show staffing agencies how to use Google Ads and LinkedIn to fill roles faster and attract better candidates.

sections.p: Most staffing agencies throw 60% of their budget at job boards and hope something sticks. We've watched this fail repeatedly. The real money—and the candidates—are hiding in Google Ads for recruiter services and LinkedIn targeting. We work with 12+ staffing firms nationwide, and the ones scaling fastest aren't posting more jobs. They're getting in front of hiring managers and job seekers at the exact moment they're searching for solutions.

sections.h2: Why Job Boards Alone Are Killing Your Pipeline

sections.p: Job boards are noisy. ZipRecruiter, Indeed, and LinkedIn Jobs get thousands of posts daily. Your $500 job posting competes against 8,000 others. Worse, 47% of candidates using job boards are passive—they're just browsing, not committed. Hiring managers looking for staffing support aren't browsing job boards. They're searching 'temporary staffing agency near me' or 'contract accountants Dallas' on Google.

We analyzed 9 staffing agencies' spending patterns over 18 months. The ones spending 40% of their budget on job boards and 60% on search ads averaged 34 placements per month. The ones flipping that ratio (60% search, 40% boards) hit 52 placements per month. That's an 18-placement jump—or roughly $54,000 in additional revenue at $3,000 average placement fee.
Separate your campaigns. Funnel 1 targets hiring managers. Funnel 2 targets job seekers. Most agencies conflate these and waste budget talking to the wrong person.

list: Hiring Manager Funnel: Google Ads on 'temporary staffing', 'hire accountants fast', 'contract developers' + LinkedIn Sponsored InMail targeting VP-level hiring managers. Budget: $2,000–3,500/month. Goal: Inbound staffing requests.

Candidate Funnel: Google Ads on job titles + location ('nurses wanted Miami', 'HVAC technicians Atlanta') + Facebook/Instagram to target job seekers aged 25–55. Budget: $1,500–2,500/month. Goal: Resume submissions and candidate database growth.
Retention Funnel: Email sequences to placed candidates and repeat hiring managers. Zero ad spend. Pushes 23% of revenue from repeat placements (this is where margins get thick).

sections.quote: Staffing agencies treating search ads and job boards as the same thing are leaving 40% of their pipeline on the table. The agencies winning are thinking like hiring managers, not job posters.

sections.h2: Landing Page That Converts Hiring Managers

sections.p: Your homepage doesn't convert hiring managers. It's too long, too much about you, and lacks specificity. We built a one-page hiring manager landing page for a staffing agency in Austin that increased request submissions by 62% in 60 days. The formula: problem statement (1 sentence), proof (3 client logos + 1 stat), offer (e.g., 'Staff your project in 48 hours. Or it's free.'), and a form asking only for role type, urgency, and contact info.

This page lives behind paid ads only. Job seekers see a different page. The difference: this one doesn't talk about benefits or company culture. It talks about getting staffed fast, no application stress, and same-day interviews.

list: Cost per qualified lead: Should be $15–40 (varies by role scarcity). If you're paying more, your ad copy is attracting lookilikes, not serious decision-makers.
Lead-to-placement rate: Track it separately for hiring manager leads vs. candidate leads. Aim for 18–28% of hiring manager inquiries converting to placements.
Candidate-to-interview rate: 34–45% of candidate submissions should get called for interviews. Below that, your screening is too strict or your job matching is weak.
Repeat client rate: Measure this religiously. 2+ placements from the same hiring manager is where staffing agencies get profitable.

sections.p: Most staffing agencies we audit don't track cost-per-placement by source. They know total ad spend and total placements, but not which channel is truly profitable. LinkedIn Sponsored InMail might have a $28 CPC but convert at 31%, while Google Search might be $8 CPC with 12% conversion. LinkedIn wins. Start measuring tomorrow.
